metadata.split_role: Expanded long-stemmed, peppery, gilled-bolete, wood-inhabiting, and oddball bolete records.
metadata.notes: Physical data split only. Species record bodies were preserved; each canonical slug appears in exactly one base mushroom file. v4.2.81 added species-specific bolete notes/source links and simplified review reasons for selected records.

review_reason_audit.rule: Normalize explicit review signals into review_reasons/review_note and prefix notes for Needs Review list visibility.

records.culinary_uses: Not treated as a food target in this guide; included mainly for identification and distribution review.
records.notes: Needs review: needs real field photo / image coverage, needs source links, and needs species-specific notes review.

mushroom_profile.summary: Distinctive mossy-hemlock bolete with a dark reddish-brown cap, yellow non-bruising pores, and a dark streaked/reticulate stem; its fit for Upper Michigan should be checked carefully.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Fall; western source material emphasizes PNW hemlock forests.
research_notes: Look for a granular to felty dark reddish-brown cap, yellow pore surface that does not bruise blue, and a dark brown to reddish-brown stem with paler streaks or coarse reticulation near the top. Often tied to mossy, decayed hemlock wood in western sources, so do not assume this is a routine U.P. bolete.
Source links and species-specific notes added in v4.2.81 mushroom batch.

manual_review_reasons: Needs real field photo / image coverage
records.edibility_detail: Not a prime food entry. Needs regional relevance confirmation before it should be treated as a normal Upper Michigan target.

records.mushroom_card_note: Distinctive mossy-hemlock bolete with a dark reddish-brown cap, yellow non-bruising pores, and a dark streaked/reticulate stem; its fit for Upper Michigan should be checked carefully.
records.field_identification: Look for a granular to felty dark reddish-brown cap, yellow pore surface that does not bruise blue, and a dark brown to reddish-brown stem with paler streaks or coarse reticulation near the top. Often tied to mossy, decayed hemlock wood in western sources, so do not assume this is a routine U.P. bolete.

records.culinary_uses: Reported edible when young and in good condition, but treat as a confident-ID bolete rather than a beginner mushroom.

mushroom_profile.summary: Minimal bolete seed entry for Admirable Bolete. Added in the Michigan audit pass; fuller ID notes, sources, and images still need review.
staining: Usually not staining; occasionally weak blue bruising on pores
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall; often July through October in the Great Lakes window.
research_notes: Species seed added from the Michigan bolete audit.
Needs image coverage.
Needs source links and fuller field notes.

records.edibility_detail: Best approached as an edible-with-caution species: confirm pine association, very long ridged stem, yellow pores, and lack of dangerous red-pored traits.
records.field_identification: Mycorrhizal with pines. Look for a reddish-brown to brown cap, yellow pore surface, very long ridged to nearly reticulate stem, prominent white basal mycelium, and a cap margin that can project beyond the pore surface.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Aureoboletus projectellus
use_links.notes: Pine association, field marks, season, and staining notes.

records.edibility_notes: Reported edible when young and in good condition, but treat as a confident-ID bolete rather than a beginner mushroom. Best approached as an edible-with-caution species: confirm pine association, very long ridged stem, yellow pores, and lack of dangerous red-pored traits.
curation_notes: Minimal species seed. Added from the Michigan bolete audit for the clean app so the bolete section is more complete; details, seasonality, and images still need a later review. Baseline audit pass added lane, seasonality, commonness, food quality, host filtering, and comparison notes.
This record still needs species-level images and source links, but the core field-guide traits are now scaffolded for sorting and filtering.

edible_use.notes: Reported edible when young and in good condition, but treat as a confident-ID bolete rather than a beginner mushroom. Best approached as an edible-with-caution species: confirm pine association, very long ridged stem, yellow pores, and lack of dangerous red-pored traits.

records.culinary_uses: Not collected for meals in this guide. Keep as an identification record until local use and species limits are checked.

mushroom_profile.summary: Minimal bolete seed entry for Aureoboletus Roxanae. Added in the Michigan audit pass; fuller ID notes, sources, and images still need review.
staining: Pores usually not bruising; flesh may stain bluish
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall; species-level local timing needs more checking.
research_notes: Species seed added from the Michigan bolete audit.
Needs image coverage.
Needs source links and fuller field notes.

records.edibility_detail: Avoid for food in this guide; keep as an identification/caution record.
records.field_identification: Dry, smooth to slightly wrinkled cap; yellow to greenish-yellow pore surface that usually does not bruise; yellowish stem with reddish colorations and tiny dots; flesh may stain bluish in some collections. Reported under hardwoods in eastern North America.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert Boletus key: Aureoboletus roxanae
use_links.notes: Hardwood association, yellow/greenish pores, dotted stem, and non-bruising pore surface.

records.edibility_notes: Yellow-pored, dotted-stem Aureoboletus/Xerocomus-type bolete. Do not infer kitchen use from nearby bolete relatives.
curation_notes: Minimal species seed. Added from the Michigan bolete audit for the clean app so the bolete section is more complete; details, seasonality, and images still need a later review. Baseline audit pass added lane, seasonality, commonness, food quality, host filtering, and comparison notes.
This record still needs species-level images and source links, but the core field-guide traits are now scaffolded for sorting and filtering.

records.culinary_uses: Not a prime food target; useful mainly as an identification species among cracked-cap and pitted/ridged-spored boletes.
records.notes: Needs review: needs real field photo / image coverage, needs source links, and needs species-specific notes review.

mushroom_profile.summary: Odd cracked-cap bolete with a brown cap, yellow pores that slowly blue, scaber-like stem texture, and a habit of appearing near decayed oak wood.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall in eastern North America.
research_notes: Check the brown to dark-brown cap that may crack, yellow pores that slowly stain blue then brown, and a stem with brown scaber-like dots or hairy/scaly patches. Often found near or from well-decayed oak wood, which is unusual for a bolete.
Source links and species-specific notes added in v4.2.81 mushroom batch.

mushroom_profile.summary: Reddish cracked-cap bolete with yellow pores that bruise blue and red-toned flesh in the stem; described from Michigan, but still a specialist ID species.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall; originally described from Michigan.
research_notes: Look for a brick-red to reddish cracked cap with pale flesh showing in the cracks, yellow pores that bruise blue then brown, and flesh that is yellow in the cap but reddish in the stem. Microscopic ridged spores separate it from look-alikes.
Source links and species-specific notes added in v4.2.81 mushroom batch.

mushroom_profile.summary: Yellow wood-associated Buchwaldoboletus with bluing flesh; keep this conservative because names in this group are tangled and wood/fungus associations matter.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Late summer to fall where recorded.
research_notes: Expect a yellow to yellow-brown bolete on or near pine wood/sawdust or associated wood-decay situations, yellow pores/stem, yellow basal mycelium, and flesh that can bruise blue. Compare carefully with B. hemichrysus and B. lignicola.
Source links and species-specific notes added in v4.2.81 mushroom batch.

mushroom_profile.summary: Peppery reddish-pored bolete like C. piperatus, but with blue bruising and an olive spore print; originally described from Michigan.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall; described from Michigan.
research_notes: Small drab pinkish-tan to reddish-brown bolete with reddish pores, yellow basal mycelium, and a peppery/bitter taste. Unlike C. piperatus, it can bruise blue and has an olive spore print.
Source links and species-specific notes added in v4.2.81 mushroom batch.

mushroom_profile.summary: Classic peppery bolete: dull reddish-brown cap, reddish pores, yellow basal mycelium, and a sharp peppery taste.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall; more common in northern/western North America.
research_notes: Look for a small drab reddish-brown to pinkish-tan bolete with brownish-red pores that darken but do not blue, yellow basal mycelium, and a very peppery taste. Usually under conifers but not exclusively.
Source links and species-specific notes added in v4.2.81 mushroom batch.

mushroom_profile.summary: Small red-pored Chalciporus with yellow flesh and pinkish-red pores; type material was collected in Michigan, but use a conservative ID standard.
mushroom_profile.season_note: July to September in reported North American material.
research_notes: Look for a small yellow to red-pink cap, red-pink pores fading yellow-orange/brown, yellow flesh that does not blue, and a yellow-based pinkish stem. Best treated as a specialist Chalciporus ID.
Source links and species-specific notes added in v4.2.81 mushroom batch.

mushroom_profile.summary: Small purple-red/red-pored bolete; useful as a comparison species among peppery/red-pored Chalciporus rather than a major edible.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer to fall where reported.
research_notes: Small convex cap often purple-red to reddish brown, red to pinkish pore surface, yellow flesh that generally does not blue, and a bare stem. Compare with C. pseudorubinellus and C. piperatus.
Source links and species-specific notes added in v4.2.81 mushroom batch.

mushroom_profile.summary: Pretty and distinctive bolete with pinkish cap, pink scabers on the stem, and a chrome-yellow stem base.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall; widely distributed east of the Great Plains.
research_notes: The key combination is pinkish to tan cap, creamy pores becoming pinkish/brownish, pinkish scabers on the stem, and a bright chrome-yellow stem base/basal mycelium. Often formerly listed as Tylopilus or Leccinum chromapes.
Source links and species-specific notes added in v4.2.81 mushroom batch.

records.edibility_detail: Edible with correct ID and fresh condition, but not prime.
records.photo_subject: Field-view fruiting body; microscopy-only images intentionally avoided.
records.mushroom_card_note: Pretty and distinctive bolete with pinkish cap, pink scabers on the stem, and a chrome-yellow stem base.
records.field_identification: The key combination is pinkish to tan cap, creamy pores becoming pinkish/brownish, pinkish scabers on the stem, and a bright chrome-yellow stem base/basal mycelium. Often formerly listed as Tylopilus or Leccinum chromapes.

records.culinary_uses: Not highlighted as a prime edible; useful mainly as a recognizable yellow-pored bolete.

mushroom_profile.summary: Clustered brown bolete with a dry brown cap, bright yellow non-bruising pores, yellow basal mycelium, and a stem often swollen above a short root.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall in eastern North America.
research_notes: Look for clustered growth, dry brown to reddish-brown cap, bright yellow pores that do not bruise blue, yellow basal mycelium, and a stem swollen near the base before tapering into a short root.
Source links and species-specific notes added in v4.2.81 mushroom batch.

records.edibility_detail: Use as an ID/comparison species unless local edible-use confidence is stronger.
records.mushroom_card_note: Clustered brown bolete with a dry brown cap, bright yellow non-bruising pores, yellow basal mycelium, and a stem often swollen above a short root.

records.culinary_uses: Not emphasized as a food mushroom; better kept as a confusing look-alike/comparison entry.

mushroom_profile.summary: Shaggy Strobilomyces species close to Old Man of the Woods; field ID is tricky and spores are the reliable separator.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall; eastern North America.
research_notes: Small erect blackish cap scales over pale gray ground, whitish pores that bruise reddish gray then black, and flesh that turns red then black. Separating it from S. floccopus / Old Man of the Woods can require spore characters.
Source links and species-specific notes added in v4.2.81 mushroom batch.
pore_color: Whitish pores bruising reddish then black

mushroom_profile.summary: Gilled bolete with bright yellow gills and white basal mycelium; compare carefully with P. rhodoxanthus, which has yellow basal mycelium.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer in eastern North America.
research_notes: Looks like a bolete from above but has gills. Fresh gills are bright yellow, and the basal mycelium should be white rather than yellow. Compare with P. rhodoxanthus; Hypomyces can obscure the stem base.
Source links and species-specific notes added in v4.2.81 mushroom batch.

boleteSubgroup: Odd ecology / wood or unusual hymenium
records.edibility_detail: Edible only with confident ID; gilled-bolete separation can be subtle.

records.culinary_uses: Often treated as edible, but keep the ID conservative because gilled bolete concepts are shifting.

mushroom_profile.summary: Common eastern gilled bolete with bright yellow gills and yellow basal mycelium; cap can be red to brown.
staining: Usually little to no bluing, occasional bluing reported
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer in eastern North America.
research_notes: Bolete-like cap and stem but with bright yellow gills. Yellow basal mycelium helps separate it from P. leucomycelinus, though Hypomyces can hide the base. Associated with hardwoods east of the Rockies.
Source links and species-specific notes added in v4.2.81 mushroom batch.

boleteSubgroup: Odd ecology / wood or unusual hymenium
records.edibility_detail: Edible with strong ID; not a beginner shortcut mushroom.
records.mushroom_card_note: Common eastern gilled bolete with bright yellow gills and yellow basal mycelium; cap can be red to brown.
records.field_identification: Bolete-like cap and stem but with bright yellow gills. Yellow basal mycelium helps separate it from P. leucomycelinus, though Hypomyces can hide the base. Associated with hardwoods east of the Rockies.

records.culinary_uses: Not a high-value edible; better as an identification/comparison species.
records.notes: Needs review: needs real field photo / image coverage, needs source links, and needs species-specific notes review.

mushroom_profile.summary: Slender hemlock-associated bolete with whitish pores that become pinkish, a long graceful stem, and a Tylopilus-like look.
staining: Variable; generally not a strong blue-stainer
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall in eastern North America.
research_notes: Long slender stem, whitish pore surface becoming pinkish to pinkish-brown, and association with hemlock or other conifers. Older guides may call it Tylopilus gracilis.
Source links and species-specific notes added in v4.2.81 mushroom batch.

records.edibility_detail: Use cautiously; not treated as a prime table mushroom here.
records.mushroom_card_note: Slender hemlock-associated bolete with whitish pores that become pinkish, a long graceful stem, and a Tylopilus-like look.
records.field_identification: Long slender stem, whitish pore surface becoming pinkish to pinkish-brown, and association with hemlock or other conifers. Older guides may call it Tylopilus gracilis.

records.culinary_uses: Not treated as a food target; useful as a Great Lakes specialty ID species.
records.notes: Needs review: needs real field photo / image coverage, needs source links, and needs species-specific notes review.

mushroom_profile.summary: Great Lakes-region oak bolete with sticky brownish cap, yellow pores that brown strongly, and nearly round spores.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall, with Great Lakes records emphasized.
research_notes: Look for a sticky yellow-brown to brown cap, yellow pore surface that bruises dark brown, a sheathing ring zone/veil remnant low on the stem, and oak association. Nearly round spores are a key confirmation feature.
Source links and species-specific notes added in v4.2.81 mushroom batch.

records.culinary_uses: Not recommended as food because it grows directly on Scleroderma earthballs, which are poisonous.
records.notes: Grows directly on earthballs rather than from plain soil or wood. Look for small yellow-brown pored caps attached to the surface of an earthball; that parasitic habit is the main field clue.

records.general_notes: Useful as a caution/curiosity species because the growth habit is distinctive: a bolete growing on another fungus.
records.edibility_detail: Avoid for food in this guide; keep as an identification/caution record.
records.field_identification: Small yellow-brown bolete that grows directly from common earthballs (Scleroderma), usually the most important ID feature. Pores are yellow to olive and the stem is small and often curved.
records.edibility_notes: The mushroom itself is sometimes described as edible, but this guide treats it as avoid/not recommended because of its poisonous earthball host.

mushroom_profile.summary: Wood-growing veined-pored Boletales oddball; keep it out of normal edible assumptions and use it as a form/comparison record.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Late summer to fall where observed.
research_notes: Look for a wood-associated fruit body with a strongly veined to merulioid underside rather than normal round bolete pores. This is not a classic cap-and-stem bolete target.
Source links and species-specific notes added in v4.2.81 mushroom batch.

mushroom_profile.summary: Bright yellow-veiled bolete when young, often with reddish/orange cap tones and blue-green bruising on pores.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall; widespread but more southern.
research_notes: Young mushrooms have a powdery bright yellow veil covering the pore surface and stem; older specimens show yellow veil remnants, reddish to orange cap colors, yellowish pores, and greenish-blue bruising.
Source links and species-specific notes added in v4.2.81 mushroom batch.

records.field_identification: Young mushrooms have a powdery bright yellow veil covering the pore surface and stem; older specimens show yellow veil remnants, reddish to orange cap colors, yellowish pores, and greenish-blue bruising.
records.edibility_detail: Avoid as a food target in this guide.

mushroom_profile.summary: Small sticky yellow-orange to reddish bolete with an unusually long, deeply reticulate stem; reported from Michigan but more southern/Appalachian in emphasis.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall; recorded from Michigan to southeastern U.S.
research_notes: Check for sticky bright yellow/orange to reddish cap, yellow non-bruising pores, and a striking long stem that is deeply ridged or lacerate-reticulate. Oak or mixed oak-pine association.
Source links and species-specific notes added in v4.2.81 mushroom batch.

mushroom_profile.summary: Eastern oak-forest bolete with sticky pinkish-brown cap and bright yellow non-bruising pores; cap surface may taste sour.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall in eastern North America.
research_notes: Look for a sticky pinkish-brown to reddish-brown cap, bright yellow pore surface that does not bruise, white basal mycelium, and slender reddish/yellowish stem. Compare with B. innixus.
Source links and species-specific notes added in v4.2.81 mushroom batch.

records.field_identification: Look for a sticky pinkish-brown to reddish-brown cap, bright yellow pore surface that does not bruise, white basal mycelium, and slender reddish/yellowish stem. Compare with B. innixus.

records.culinary_uses: Not recommended as food; included for odd wood-associated bolete recognition.

mushroom_profile.summary: Wood-associated Buchwaldoboletus tied to conifer wood and possibly Phaeolus schweinitzii; keep as an oddball ID record.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall where recorded.
research_notes: Yellow-brown cap, yellow pore surface, wood association, and possible relationship with the dyer’s polypore Phaeolus schweinitzii are the useful clues. Compare with B. hemichrysus and B. sphaerocephalus.
Source links and species-specific notes added in v4.2.81 mushroom batch.
